content:
Consider the following two relations, R(A, B) and S(A, C):

R
AB
1020
2030
3040
3050
5095
S
AC
1090
3045
4080

The total number of tuples obtained by evaluating the following expression

$$ \sigma_{B < C}(R \bowtie_{R.A = S.A} S) $$

is _________
explanation:
The given relational algebra expression is:

$$ \sigma_{B < C}(R \bowtie_{R.A = S.A} S) $$

Let's break down the steps to solve this expression and find the total number of tuples obtained:

1. Compute the natural join $ R \bowtie_{R.A = S.A} S $:

The natural join $ R \bowtie_{R.A = S.A} S $ will combine the tuples from $ R $ and $ S $ where the values of attribute $ A $ are equal. This results in the following combined tuples:

A B C
102090
304045
305045

The combined tuples are:

  • (10, 20, 90)
  • (30, 40, 45)
  • (30, 50, 45)

2. Apply the selection $ \sigma_{B < C} $:

We need to select tuples where the value of attribute $ B $ is less than the value of attribute $ C $. Let's apply this condition to each tuple:

  • For (10, 20, 90): $ 20 < 90 $ is true
  • For (30, 40, 45): $ 40 < 45 $ is true
  • For (30, 50, 45): $ 50 < 45 $ is false

The selected tuples are:

  • (10, 20, 90)
  • (30, 40, 45)

3. Count the total number of tuples:

The total number of selected tuples is 2.

Therefore, the total number of tuples obtained by evaluating the expression is:

$$ 2 $$

Consider the following three relations in a relational database.

Employee ( $$\underline {eld} $$ , Name), Brand ( $$\underline {bld} $$ , bName), Own ( $$\underline {eld} $$ , $$\underline {bld} $$)

Which of the following relational algebra expressions return the set of elds who own all the brands?
explanation:
$$\bullet$$ $$\pi$$eld, bld (Own) / $$\pi$$bld (Brand) $$\Rightarrow$$ Results eid's which owns every brand of brand relation.

$$\bullet$$ Option (b) expansion and division using basic operators.

content: A relation r(A, B) in a relational database has 1200 tuples. The attribute A has integer values ranging from 6 to 20, and the attribute B has integer values ranging from 1 to 20. Assume that the attributes A and B are independently distributed. The estimated number of tuples in the output of σ(A>10)∨(B=18)(r) is ______
explanation:
To estimate the number of tuples resulting from the selection $\sigma_{(A>10) \lor (B=18)}(r)$, we can use the concept of selectivity and the given that attributes $ A $ and $ B $ are independently distributed.

Step 1: Determine the selectivity of each condition individually.

Selectivity of $ A > 10 $:

Possible values of $ A $ range from 6 to 20, totaling 15 distinct values.

Values where $ A > 10 $ are from 11 to 20, totaling 10 values.

Selectivity $ P(A > 10) = \dfrac{\text{Number of values where } A > 10}{\text{Total number of } A \text{ values}} = \dfrac{10}{15} = \dfrac{2}{3} $.

Selectivity of $ B = 18 $:

Possible values of $ B $ range from 1 to 20, totaling 20 distinct values.

There's only 1 value where $ B = 18 $.

Selectivity $ P(B = 18) = \dfrac{\text{Number of values where } B = 18}{\text{Total number of } B \text{ values}} = \dfrac{1}{20} $.

Selectivity of $ A > 10 $ and $ B = 18 $ (since $ A $ and $ B $ are independent):

$ P(A > 10 \text{ and } B = 18) = P(A > 10) \times P(B = 18) = \dfrac{2}{3} \times \dfrac{1}{20} = \dfrac{1}{30} $.

Step 2: Compute the combined selectivity using the inclusion-exclusion principle.

$ P((A > 10) \lor (B = 18)) = P(A > 10) + P(B = 18) - P(A > 10 \text{ and } B = 18) $

Plugging in the values:

$ P((A > 10) \lor (B = 18)) = \dfrac{2}{3} + \dfrac{1}{20} - \dfrac{1}{30} $

Convert all fractions to a common denominator (60):

$ P((A > 10) \lor (B = 18)) = \dfrac{40}{60} + \dfrac{3}{60} - \dfrac{2}{60} = \dfrac{41}{60} $

Step 3: Estimate the number of tuples in the output.

$ \text{Estimated number of tuples} = \text{Total tuples} \times P((A > 10) \lor (B = 18)) = 1200 \times \dfrac{41}{60} = 820 $

Answer:

820

We define $${r_1} = {\pi _{A,B,C}}\left( r \right)$$ and $${r_1} = {\pi _{A,D}}\left( r \right)$$. Let $$s = {r_1}*{r_2}$$ where * denotes natural join. Given that the decomposition of r into r1 and r2 is lossy, which one of the following is TRUE?

Let R<sub>1</sub> (<u>A,</u> B, C) and R<sub>2</sub> (<u>D,</u> E) be two relation schema, where the primary keys are shown underlined, and let C be a foreign key in R<sub>1</sub> referring to R<sub>2</sub>. Suppose there is no violation of the above referential integrity constraint in the corresponding relation instances r<sub>1</sub> and r<sub>2</sub>. Which one of the following relational algebra expressions would necessarily produce an empty relation?

Select distinct a1, a2, ..., an 

From r1, r2, ..., rm 

Where P;
For an arbitrary predicate P, this query is equivalent to which of the following relational algebra expressions?
With regard to the expressive power of the formal relational query languages, which of the following statements is true?

options.content: Relational algebra is more powerful than relational calculus.
Relational algebra has the same power as relational calculus.
Relational algebra has the same power as safe relational calculus.

employee (name, salary, deptno), and
department (deptno, deptname, address)

Which of the following queries cannot be expressed using the basic relational algebra operations $$\left(\sigma, \pi,\times ,\Join, \cup, \cap,-\right)$$?

options.content: Employees whose name is the same as their department name
The sum of all employee’s salaries
All employees of a given department

content: Consider the join of a relation R with a relation S. If R has m tuples and S has n tuples then the maximum and minimum sizes of the join respectively are

The relational algebra expression equivalent to the following tuple calculus expression:

$$\left\{ {t|t \in r \wedge \left( {t\left[ A \right] = 10 \wedge t\left[ B \right] = 20} \right)} \right\}$$ is:
Give a relational algebra expression using only the minimum number of operators from $$\left( { \cup ,\, - } \right)$$ which is equivalent to $$R \cap S$$.
explanation:
$$R \cap S = R - \left( {R - S} \right)$$

Intersection can be expressed in terms of union and minus.

But we don't need to use both of them as in the question says use only the minimum number of operators from $$\left( { \cup ,\, - } \right)$$ to create a relational algebra expression which is equivalent to $$R \cap S$$. So we use only minus( - ) operator.

content: An instance of a relational scheme R(A, B, C) has distinct values for attribute A. Can you conclude that A is a candidate key for R?

Consider a relational database schema with two relations $R(P, Q)$ and $S(X, Y)$.

Let $E=\{\langle u\rangle \mid \exists v \exists w\langle u, v\rangle \in R \wedge\langle v, w\rangle \in S\}$ be a tuple relational calculus expression. Which one of the following relational algebraic expressions is equivalent to $E$ ?
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} & R(P, Q) S(X, Y) \\ & E=\{\langle u\rangle \mid \exists v \exists w\langle u, v\rangle \in R \wedge\langle v, w\rangle \in S\} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \Pi_P\left(S \bowtie \bowtie_{S . X=R . Q} R\right) $$

content:
Consider two relations describing teams and players in a sports league:

$\bullet$ teams(tid, tname): tid, tname are team-id and team-name, respectively.

$\bullet$ players(pid, pname, tid): pid, pname, and tid denote player-id, player-name and the team-id of the player, respectively.

Which ONE of the following tuple relational calculus queries returns the name of the players who play for the team having tname as ' $M I$ '?

options.content: $\{$ p.pname $\mid p \in$ players $\wedge \exists t(t \in$ teams $\wedge$ p.tid $=$ t.tid $\wedge$ t.name $=' \mathrm{MI}$ ' $)\}$
$\{$ p.pname $\mid p \in$ teams $\wedge \exists t(t \in$ players $\wedge$ p.tid $=$ t.tid $\wedge$ t.name $=' \mathrm{Ml}$ ') \}
$\{$ p.pname $\mid p \in$ players $\wedge \exists t(t \in$ teams $\wedge$ t.name $=' M 1$ ' $)\}$
$\{p . p n a m e \mid p \in$ teams $\wedge \exists t(t \in$ players $\wedge$ t.name $=$ ' Ml ' $)\}$

explanation:
We want the names of players (from the players relation) who are on the team whose tname is 'MI' (from the teams relation). Let’s examine each proposed TRC query in turn:

Relation Definitions Recap

teams(tid, tname)

players(pid, pname, tid)

Here,

tid is the team's ID.

tname is the team's name (e.g., 'MI').

players.tid is a foreign key referencing teams.tid.

Understanding the Correct Logic

To find player names of the team named 'MI', we want:

Start from players: for each tuple $p \in \text{players}$.

Join with teams: require that there exists a team $t \in \text{teams}$ such that

$p.\text{tid} = t.\text{tid}$

$t.\text{tname} = \text{'MI'}$.

Project $p.\text{pname}$.

So in standard TRC form:

$ \{\,p.\mathrm{pname} \mid p \in \mathrm{players} \,\wedge\, \exists\,t \,\bigl(t\in \mathrm{teams} \,\wedge\, p.\mathrm{tid}=t.\mathrm{tid} \,\wedge\, t.\mathrm{tname}=\text{'MI'}\bigr)\}. $

Checking the Provided Options

Let’s match each option against the logic above (noting that a small mismatch like using t.name vs t.tname can be a shorthand but is structurally the same idea if everything else fits).

Option A

$ \bigl\{\;p.\mathrm{pname}\;\mid\;p\in \mathrm{players}\;\wedge\;\exists t\,(\,t\in \mathrm{teams}\;\wedge\;p.\mathrm{tid}=t.\mathrm{tid}\;\wedge\;t.\mathrm{name}='\mathrm{MI}'\,)\bigr\}. $

This says: “From players pick those p for which there exists some t in teams with matching tid and t.name = 'MI'.”

Aside from the label t.name vs t.tname, the structure exactly matches our needed join-and-filter.

Logically correct for returning player names of team 'MI'.

Option B

$ \{\,p.\mathrm{pname} \mid p\in \mathrm{teams} \,\wedge\, \exists t(\,t\in \mathrm{players}\,\wedge\, p.\mathrm{tid}=t.\mathrm{tid}\,\wedge\,t.\mathrm{name}='\mathrm{MI}')\}. $

Here, $p$ is taken from teams, but then we try to project p.pname. However, teams only has attributes (tid, tname)—it does not have a pname attribute.

Also, t.name='MI' is interpreted as checking if a player’s name is 'MI', not the team’s.

Incorrect for our goal.

Option C

$ \{\,p.\mathrm{pname} \mid p\in \mathrm{players}\,\wedge\, \exists t(\,t\in \mathrm{teams}\,\wedge\, t.\mathrm{name}='\mathrm{MI}')\}. $

This only says “there exists some team named 'MI',” without joining p.tid to t.tid. Essentially all players would match if any team named 'MI' exists.

Incorrect because it doesn’t ensure the player belongs to the 'MI' team.

Option D

$ \{\,p.\mathrm{pname}\mid p\in \mathrm{teams}\,\wedge\, \exists t(t\in \mathrm{players}\,\wedge\, t.\mathrm{name}='\mathrm{MI}')\}. $

Same structural problems as (B): it takes p from teams (which lacks pname) and never ties the team to the 'MI' entity in a meaningful way.

Incorrect.

Conclusion

Only Option A has the correct structure to start with players, join on tid, and require the team’s name be 'MI'.

Hence, the correct answer is

$ \boxed{\text{Option A.}} $

content:
The relation schema, Person($\underline{\text{pid}}$, $city$), describes the city of residence for every person uniquely identified by $pid$. The following relational algebra operators are available: selection, projection, cross product, and rename.

To find the list of cities where at least 3 persons reside, using the above operators, the minimum number of cross product operations that must be used is
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} R_1 &\leftarrow \sigma(R \times S) \\ &\quad \text{with condition: } R.city = S.city \wedge R.pid \neq S.pid \\\\ R_2 &\leftarrow \sigma(R_1 \times P) \\ &\quad \text{with condition: } R_1.city = P.city \wedge R_1.pid \neq P.pid \\\\ R_3 &\leftarrow \pi(R_2) \\ &\quad \text{city} \end{aligned} $$

Thus, we require 3 tables and 2 cross product operations. Therefore, the correct option is (B).

content:
Consider the following relations P(X,Y,Z), Q(X,Y,T) and R(Y,V).

How many tuples will be returned by the following relational algebra query?
explanation:
The first query returns-

$$ \begin{array}{|c|} \hline X \\ \hline X_2 \\ \hline \end{array} $$

Second query returns-

$$ \begin{array}{|c|} \hline X \\ \hline X_1 \\ \hline \end{array} $$

Difference will return -

$$ \begin{array}{|c|} \hline X \\ \hline X_2 \\ \hline \end{array} $$

Hence , the correct answer is 1.

content:
Consider the relations $$r(A, B)$$ and $$s(B, C),$$ where $$s.B$$ is a primary key and $$r.B$$ is a foreign key referencing $$s.B.$$ Consider the query

$$Q:$$ $$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,$$ $$r$$ $$\,\,\,\,$$ $$\bowtie$$ $$\,\,\,\,$$ $$\left( {{\sigma _{b < 5}}\left( s \right)} \right)$$

Let $$LOJ$$ denote the natural left outer-join operation. Assume that $$r$$ and $$s$$ contain no null values.

Which one of the following queries is NOT equivalent to $$Q$$?
explanation:
Given : $r(A, B)$ and $s(B, C)$

Query : Q : r $\bowtie\left(\sigma_B<5(\mathrm{~s})\right)$

Example :

Query : Q : $\mathrm{r} \bowtie\left(\sigma_{\mathrm{B}}<(\mathrm{s})\right)$

Result in :

$$ \begin{array}{|c|c|c|} \hline \mathrm{A} & \mathrm{~B} & \mathrm{C} \\ \hline a_1 & 2 & c_1 \\ \hline a_2 & 4 & c_1 \\ \hline a_3 & 4 & c_1 \\ \hline \end{array} $$

Consider Query $(\mathrm{A}): \sigma_{\mathrm{B} < S}(\mathrm{r} \bowtie \mathrm{s})$

Result in :

$$ \begin{array}{|c|c|c|} \hline \mathrm{A} & \mathrm{~B} & \mathrm{C} \\ \hline a_1 & 2 & c_1 \\ \hline a_2 & 4 & c_1 \\ \hline a_3 & 4 & c_1 \\ \hline \end{array} $$

Consider Query (B) : $\sigma_{\mathrm{B}<\mathrm{s}}(\mathrm{r}⟕ \mathrm{s})$

Result in :

$$ \begin{array}{|c|c|c|} \hline \mathrm{A} & \mathrm{~B} & \mathrm{C} \\ \hline a_1 & 2 & c_1 \\ \hline a_2 & 4 & c_1 \\ \hline a_3 & 4 & c_1 \\ \hline \end{array} $$

Consider Query (C) : $\mathrm{r} ⟕ \sigma_{\mathrm{B}<5}(\mathrm{~s})$ )

Result in :

$$ \begin{array}{|c|c|c|} \hline \mathrm{A} & \mathrm{~B} & \mathrm{C} \\ \hline a_1 & 2 & c_1 \\ \hline a_2 & 4 & c_1 \\ \hline a_3 & 4 & c_1 \\ \hline a_4 & 6 & \text { NULL } \\ \hline a_5 & 6 & \text { NULL } \\ \hline \end{array} $$

Consider Query (D) : $\sigma_B < (\mathrm{r}) ⟕ \mathrm{s}$

Result in :

$$ \begin{array}{|c|c|c|} \hline \mathrm{A} & \mathrm{~B} & \mathrm{C} \\ \hline a_1 & 2 & c_1 \\ \hline a_2 & 4 & c_1 \\ \hline a_3 & 4 & c_1 \\ \hline \end{array} $$

So Query (C) not equal to given Query Q.

Hence, the correct option is (C)

content:
Consider two relations $${R_1}\left( {A,B} \right)$$ with the tuples $$(1,5), (3,7)$$ and $${R_2}\left( {A,C} \right) = \left( {1,7} \right),\left( {4,9} \right).$$
Assume that $$R(A,B,C)$$ is the full natural outer join of $${R_1}$$ and $${R_2}$$. Consider the following tuples of the form $$(A,B,C): a = (1,5,null),$$ $$b = (1,null,7),$$ $$c = (3, null, 9),$$ $$d = (4,7,null),$$ $$e = (1,5,7),$$ $$f = (3,7,null),$$ $$g = (4,null,9).$$ Which one of the following statements is correct?

options.content: $$R$$ contains $$a, b, e, f, g$$ but not $$c, d.$$
$$R$$ contains all of $$a, b, c, d, e, f, g.$$
$$R$$ contains $$e, f, g$$ but not $$a, b.$$

content: Consider a join (relation algebra) between relations r(R)and s(S) using the nested loop method. There are 3 buffers each of size equal to disk block size, out of which one buffer is reserved for intermediate results. Assuming size(r(R)) < size(s(S)), the join will have fewer number of disk block accesses if

options.content: relation r(R) is in the outer loop.
relation s(S) is in the outer loop.
join selection factor between r(R) and s(S) is more than 0.5.
join selection factor between r(R) and s(S) is less than 0.5.

Consider a relational table r with sufficient number of records, having attributes A1, A2,....., An and let 1 $$ \le $$ p $$ \le $$ n. Two queries Q1 and Q2 are given below.

$$Q1: \pi_{A_1, \dots ,A_p} \left(\sigma_{A_p=c}\left(r\right)\right)$$ where is a constant

$$Q2: \pi_{A_1, \dots ,A_p} \left(\sigma_{c_1 \leq A_p \leq c_2}\left(r\right)\right)$$ where c1 and c2 are constants

The database can be configured to do ordered indexing on Ap or hashing on Ap. Which of the following statements is TRUE?

options.content: Ordered indexing will always outperform hashing for both queries
Hashing will always outperform ordered indexing for both queries

content:
The following functional dependencies hold for relations R(A, B, C) and S(B, D, E): $$$\eqalign{ & B \to A \cr & A \to C \cr} $$$

The relation R contains 200 tuples and the relation S contains 100 tuples. What is the maximum number of tuples possible in the natural join R $$\Join$$ S?

Let R and S be two relations with the following schema

R (P, Q, R1, R2, R3)

S (P, Q, S1, S2)

Where {P, Q} is the key for both schemas. Which of the following queries are equivalent?
I. $$\Pi_P \left(R \bowtie S\right)$$
II. $$\Pi_P \left(R\right) \bowtie \Pi_P\left(S\right)$$
III. $$\Pi_P \left(\Pi_{P, Q} \left(R\right) \cap \Pi_{P,Q} \left(S\right) \right)$$
IV. $$\Pi_P \left(\Pi_{P, Q} \left(R\right) - \left(\Pi_{P,Q} \left(R\right) - \Pi_{P,Q} \left(S\right)\right)\right)$$

Information about a collection of students is given by the relation studInfo(studId, name, sex). The relation enroll(studId, courseId) gives which student has enrolled for (or taken) what course(s). Assume that every course is taken by at least one male and at least one female student. What does the following relational algebra expression represent?

$$\eqalign{ & \prod\nolimits_{courseId} {((\prod\nolimits_{studId} {({\sigma _{sex = 'female'}}} } \cr & (studInfo)) \times \prod\nolimits_{courseId} {(enroll)) - enroll)} \cr} $$

options.content: Courses in which all the female students are enrolled.
Courses in which a proper subset of female students are enrolled.
Courses in which only male students are enrolled.

content:
Consider the relation employee(name, sex, supervisorName) with name as the key, supervisorName gives the name of the supervisor of the employee under consideration. What does the following Tuple Relational Calculus query produce?

$$\eqalign{ & \{ e.name\,|\,employee(e) \wedge \cr & (\forall x)[\neg employee(x) \vee \cr & x.\sup ervisorName \ne e.name\, \vee \cr & x.sex = 'male']\} \cr} $$

options.content: Names of employees with a male supervisor.
Names of employees with no immediate male subordinates.
Names of employees with no immediate female subordinates.
Names of employees with a female supervisor.

content: Which of the following query transformations (i.e. replacing the l.h.s. expression by the r.h.s. expression) is incorrect? R<sub>1</sub> and R<sub>2</sub> are relations, C<sub>1</sub>, C<sub>2</sub> are selection conditions and A<sub>1</sub>, A<sub>2</sub> are attributes of R<sub>1</sub>?

A library relational database system uses the following schema

USERS (User #, User Name, Home Town)

BOOKS (Books # Book Title, Author Name)

ISSUED (Book #, User #, Date)

Explain in one English sentence, what each of the following relational algebra queries is designed to determine.

(a) $$\sigma_{ \text{User#}=6}\left(\pi_{\text{User#, Book Title}}\left(\left(\text{USERS} \bowtie \text{ISSUED}\right) \bowtie \text{BOOKS}\right)\right)$$

(b) $$\pi_{\text{Author Name}}\left(\text{BOOKS} \bowtie \sigma_{\text{Home Town=Delhi}} \left(\text{USERS} \bowtie \text{ISSUED}\right)\right)$$
answer:
(a) Select the titles of the books issued to User# = 6

(b) Select author names of the books issued to users who are from Delhi.
